steps.title: Spot the disqualifying caveat
steps.content: The researchers explicitly flag their supplementation data as “rare, highly varied, and poorly tracked”—which makes these findings unreliable.

steps.title: Match the cautioned conclusion
steps.content: Choice C claims that supplementation has “no promise” as a strategy. That conclusion rests entirely on the supplementation data—which the researchers have identified as compromised. They caution against drawing such a definitive claim from flawed evidence. Choices A and D address separate, well-supported observations; B describes what the researchers actually infer from the data problems. That matches choice C — C is correct.
